document: Operational Guidelines for Open Banking in Nigeria (Nigeria - Row 9)

sections.summary: This section establishes anti-money laundering (AML) and counter-financing of terrorism (CFT) requirements for open banking in Nigeria. It mandates that all open banking participants must implement robust AML/CFT measures to prevent illicit financial activities. These obligations apply to financial institutions, payment service providers, and other entities involved in open banking transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires open banking participants to implement AML/CFT safeguards, which are critical for DTIAs to ensure compliance with financial crime prevention when transferring data across borders. It directly impacts cross-border data transfers by mandating robust A:ML/CFT checks.

requirements.text: All open banking participants must implement robust AML/CFT measures to prevent illicit financial activities.

sections.source_quote: ANTI-MONEY LAUNDERING (AML) AND COMBATING THE FINANCING OF TERRORISM (CFT) .. 23

sections.summary: This section establishes key terms and acronyms for the Open Banking ecosystem in Nigeria, including definitions for API Provider, API Consumer, Two Factor Authentication, Financial Institution, and other critical concepts. It clarifies the roles of participants in open banking and specifies technical standards for data handling and system configuration. The definitions ensure clarity and consistency in how financial institutions and technology providers interact within Nigeria's open banking framework. API providers and consumers must determine incident scope and impact, notify affected parties, assess failover systems, implement workarounds, evaluate cost efficiency, investigate root causes, and follow documented procedures for resolution. They must also create and test incident response plans, provide 24x7 monitoring, maintain incident manuals with lessons learned, and ensure quarterly reviews of incident management protocols. These obligations apply to all API providers and consumers participating in Nigeria's Open Banking API infrastructure. This section mandates that Account Providers (APs) and Account Custodians (ACs) must only share customer information with each other upon
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical terms for data sharing in open banking, ensuring clarity on participant roles and technical standards that impact data transfer mechanisms and security requirements for cross-border financial data exchanges. This section mandates specific incident response procedures and documentation requirements that directly impact data transfer impact assessments by establishing critical thresholds for service disruption, failover timelines, and transparency in incident handling across international API ecosystems. This section requires explicit customer consent for data sharing between AP

Determine the scope and impact of the incident and notify API providers/consumers relying on services via the recommended communication channel
Assess the functionality and reliability of the failover system
Consider workarounds to restore service in a timely manner
Evaluate the cost efficiency of the failover compared with the loss experienced from the incident
Investigate root cause and commence resolution following documented procedures
Create and regularly test an incident response plan
Provide skilled personnel to monitor and support Open Banking API infrastructure on 24x7 basis
Provide contacts of the relevant personnel to API providers/consumers within SLA documentation
Design and review on a quarterly basis an incident management and response manual indicating failover and failback steps for a critical incident
Maintain an incident manual with lessons learnt from previous critical incidents
APs shall only share information of a customer with an AC upon presentation of a valid proof of consent by the customer
APs shall authenticate such consent to ensure it emanates from its customer

For consent obtained from a customer to be valid, the AC must present specific information including full legal name, business registration number, access level, duration of consent, frequency of access, and withdrawal process
The consent must explicitly state the type of access the AC shall have on the customer account in line with access level by data and service category
The consent must specify the duration/tenor of the consent or the date when the access shall be invalidated
The consent must indicate the frequency of access to the customer information or if such access shall be one-off
The consent must include whether the request covers the customer's consent to collect data for anonymous/de-identified data analysis
The consent must provide information on the process for withdrawal of consent by the customer, including a statement that customers can withdraw consent at any time
The consent must detail the process for withdrawal of consent by the customer
The consent must outline the consequences of withdrawal of consent to the customer, if any
The consent must include information on the AC's policy for deletion or de-identification of redundant data in accordance with extant laws and regulations
The consent must provide the customer with rights to elect for deletion of their redundant data and information on how to exercise such rights
The AP shall verify that the consent emanated from the customer using Two Factor Authentication (2FA)
The request for customer data must contain the purpose of the request
The request must include the credentials of the requesting AC
The request must contain a valid date and be made through appropriate channels
The AP shall create a token reflecting the details of the rights granted to the AC by the customer
The token shall be encrypted and securely exchanged with the AC
All responses of the AP shall be in real-time
For every API call to read customer information or conduct transactions, the AP shall validate the token to ensure it continues to meet the rights granted by the customer
The customer shall be informed of type and purpose of data requested, terms and conditions applicable, in concise and easy to understand manner
The consent shall be explicit
The customer shall be presented with the option to accept or decline onboarding into the Open Banking arrangement
In the consent stage, customer shall be shown the requested information and purpose
The consent provided shall be time-bound, not in perpetuity
A customer shall be allowed to explicitly opt-out, which shall be provided to customer periodically
Customer after providing consent, shall be accurately informed of the time-bound permission provided
Authentication shall provide a mechanism to verify user identity to the APs
APs shall provide authentication mechanisms in acceptable form that conform to pre-authorized channels (email, phone numbers, devices, applications, biometrics etc)
User endpoint should be verified prior to use for authentication (emails, phone numbers or other user endpoints shall be verified using control information such as OTP verification)
Appropriate customer authentication methods such as multi-factor authentication shall be established to reduce the chance of identity theft or fraud
ACs shall provide mechanisms for customer to immediately enable or revoke permissions to different particulars of each account
Each change in the user's selection shall be proceeded with the generation of a new OAuth token to capture the change in scope
When prompting users for consent on the application, details shall be grouped for ease of presentation
Sufficient information in the confirmation screen shall capture or make available the details involved
Details shall be presented in friendly and easily understandable language that embodies the object, action, initiator (user or auto) and periodicity
Notifications shall be provided, using agreed channels, to customers indicating any changes to permissions requested against AP for the user's keepsake
The customer shall be presented with the details about the consent required on the AC-user interface
The customer is presented with the options to allow or deny the request from participants to access the shown data
The response from the end-user is then sent to the AP, and the data must be recorded accordingly
Participant shall provide a minimum of two (2) channels by which the end-user can view, consent, decline or revoke permission requests

When consent is given by the end-user for specific permissions, the records of such consent shall be recorded along with a timestamp, an audit record capturing a transcript of the change in permissions scope and the point of initiation
Revocations shall be captured with a timestamp, an audit record capturing a transcript of the change in permissions scope and the point of initiation
Consented scopes shall be updated within the AP's API-directory
Subsequent token requests by the ACs on behalf of the user shall include consented scopes

The AP may provide additional services to notify the AC of the approved scopes so the AC can proactively enable those functions within the customer's profile instead of waiting for the next sign-on event
Participants shall comply with all CBN regulations relating to open banking and ensure that third-party service providers also comply.
Participants, shall at the minimum: Adopt/implement a regulatory risk framework
Participants, shall at the minimum: Adopt comprehensive internal controls, including adequate policies, procedures, and limits
Participants, shall at the minimum: Insurance policy to cover losses
Participants, shall at the minimum: Have a dedicated compliance officer
Participants, shall at the minimum: Develop compliance policy/manual
Participants shall put measures in place to guarantee integrity and availability of data
Participants, shall at the minimum: Implement full audit trail of all transactions
Participants, shall at the minimum: Implement access rules, approvals, revocations and review procedures
Data shall follow standards such as the 9 ALCOA+ principle of Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, and Accurate, Complete, Consistent, Enduring, and Available
Participants shall put measures in place to guarantee confidentiality of data and comply with extant laws and regulations on data protection and privacy
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: A robust ISMS to ensure sensitive data are systematically managed
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: A Data loss prevention (DLP) procedure
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: Encryption and pseudonymization procedure
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: Identity and access management (IDAM) procedure
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: The creation of a dispute resolution mechanism/body specific to open banking including customer liability framework
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: Know your data
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: Separation of duties within participants on access to customer data; 2 level accessibility to customer data
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: An adherence to information security standards and data protection regulations
Participants, shall at the minimum, implement: Development of technology standards for participants in open banking ecosystem
Participants shall ensure that all products are approved by the regulator
Participants shall ensure that risks associated with the products are identified and risk mitigants are put in place
Participants shall ensure that the benefits to the customers are known
Participants shall implement zero-trust architecture.
Maintain minimal user access rights in accordance with business function, process requirements and security policies.
Align the management of identities and access rights to the defined roles and responsibilities, based on least-privilege, need-to-have and need-to-know principles.
Ensure that access authentication to information assets shall be based on the individual's role or business rules.
Coordinate with business units that manage authentication within applications used in business processes to ensure that authentication controls have been properly administered.
The Biometric Verification Number (BVN), National Identity Number (NIN) and Tax Identification Number and other such unique identifiers, shall be stored in independent identity systems.
Each endpoint accessing an open banking product and service shall be uniquely identified and its attributes examined for compliance of information security policies.
The process through which an end user authenticates itself to its service provider shall be designed to minimize digital friction.
Participants shall use strong authentication which includes Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) to manage access to API systems and implement role-based access control for PIFT and PAST.
Participants shall adopt authentication protocols at the minimum, OAuth 2.0 in conjunction with OpenID Connect. Higher authentication protocols shall be implemented as required.
Mutual authentication over Transport Layer Security (mTLS) shall be implemented for authentication between client and server machines or any approved authentication standard.
Implement open banking security profiles in compliance with the Financial-grade API (FAPI) specifications.
Implement digital tokens that ensures third party is acting within the bounds of the permissions granted.
Provide evidence that their third-party service is entitled to use an authorisation token such as providing client identity and client secret to provider.
Be responsible for identity token management and ensure that third-party providers are in possession of legitimate tokens.
Ensure that access granted to third-party providers are defined in terms of specific permissions to data and/or functionality.
Note that all malicious misuse of permissions is prohibited.

Document and implement the following: Customer revocation rights, a mechanism through which users can review and cancel their permissions, assignment of risk levels to permissions, prohibitions on granting permissions, placement of contextual limits on permissions, subject permissions to time/duration limits, refresh and challenge permission token validity at specific intervals.
Define roles with appropriate permissions and be defined in a standardized nomenclature for future work.
Participants shall secure identities propagated across all channels and storage systems.
Implement anti-fraud and counter terrorism financing controls and capabilities.
Ensure that the API provides supports out-of-band (OOB) authentication, where necessary;
Be required to notify the user asynchronously/OOB when significant actions have occurred such as, a change to a payee etc;
Ensure that the API responses inform the third-party and customer that an OOB process is underway so that, where appropriate;
Ensure that fraud-relevant information, such as IP addresses and traffic information, in the API return messages are stored and investigated for research and development purposes.

Participants shall assign roles and responsibilities for identifying and monitoring any changes in legal, regulatory, and other external contractual requirements relevant to the use of IT resources and the processing of open banking information within the business and IT operations.
All participants shall implement updated anti-malware and anti-ransomware solutions automatically or at least semi-automatically.
Apply data classification and labelling techniques to protect sensitive data and information assets at rest, in motion and in use;

Protect personal and transactional data against data loss, theft and breach in line with regulations such as the Nigeria Data Protection Regulation (NDPR) and/or any data protection regulation issued by the Bank;
Have and implement clearly defined data retention and destruction policy(ies) in line with CBN regulations, the Nigerian Data Protection Regulations, etc.

Integrate the planning, design, implementation and monitoring of information security and privacy procedures and other controls capable of enabling prompt detection, prevention of security events, and response to security incidents;
Ensure that cyber risks are thoroughly investigated, and root causes communicated with additional risk response requirements and process improvements to appropriate decision makers;

Implement corrective actions based on root causes such as problems with business process, application system development and maintenance, development and test environments, documentation and manuals, and data integrity;
Establish Cyber-Threat Intelligence (CTI) capability to detect, prevent, and respond to potential cyber-threats and risks, as well as develop relevant CTI policies;
Report and share cyber security intelligence and threat feeds to the CBN Security Operation Centre or any such centre as may be required by the CBN.
Implement digital forensics capabilities for open banking operations.

Implement network security controls to proactively detect, prevent and respond to network threats that can affect the confidentiality, integrity and availability of open banking information and assets;
Adequately and securely configure open banking gateways to minimize integration friction and enable seamless information flow;
Implement Transport Layer Security (TLS) for network communication channels for open banking platforms with extended validation certificates.
Conduct periodic vulnerability scanning and penetration testing of system security to determine adequacy of system protection against cyber risks.
Remediate identified vulnerabilities in tandem to the severity of the open banking assets.
Ensure that changes to open banking product and services are made in a secured, structured manner and controlled environment;

Change controls include impact analysis on business process, infrastructure, systems and applications, business continuity plans (BCPs) and service providers to ensure that all affected components have been identified;

All emergency changes are monitored, verified, and post-implementation reviews conducted involving all concerned parties. The review shall consider and initiate corrective actions based on root causes such as problems with business process, application system development and maintenance, development and test environments, documentation and manuals, and data integrity;

Change status reports with performance metrics are implemented to enable management review and monitoring of both the detailed status of changes and the overall state such as age analysis of change requests, which shall form audit trail.
Ensure a documented inventory of configuration items that relate to open banking operations which include applications, devices, endpoints, platforms, staff, personnel, data etc;
Document and regularly review all configuration changes on open banking infrastructure;
Ensure documentation of configuration item changes using standardised version control formats such as Semantic Versioning 2.0.0;
Enforce continuous patching, including documenting and archiving dates to all technology and have individual accountability for patching specific software and equipment.
Implement and regularly review Disaster Recovery (DR) and Business Continuity (BC) policies, procedures, and controls to ensure resilience of open banking operations.
Implement backup controls and regularly restore to reduce service unavailability.
Implement controls such as Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) controls on open banking environment, to ensure resilience and 99.99 per cent availability.
Participants shall ensure that open banking data are retained for reference for a minimum of ten years.

Implement controls that audit all activities and generate tamper-proof digital logs that can used as court evidence and non-disputable audit trail for effective problem resolution and forensics examination;
Implement monitoring controls to detect, prevent and respond to anomalous activities on open banking IT operations;
Implement threat monitoring, alerting systems and intelligence to review and refine threats and implement mitigating actions regularly for continuous improvement.
Implement security controls into the Software Development Lifecyle (SDLC) for all open banking APIs;
Embed information security from planning, designing to implementation of all open banking software products;
Ensure strong IT systems controls (access and role management etc.) covering Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC), continuous integration, and continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines;
Secure APIs and web application security risks in line with recommendations of the OWASP API Security project and OWASP Top Ten Web Application Security Risks respectively.

Ensure that practices considered inimical to the open banking ecosystem, are not carried out. Such practices include screen scraping, shotting, copying, snipping and others as may be determined by the Bank from time to time.
Safeguard their customers' data in line with NDPR and/or any CBN regulation on data privacy and protection and enforce the requirements with their third parties
Not use, access, or store any data for purposes other than the service explicitly requested by the end user
Enable the customer to exercise their right to data portability
Inaugurate a problem register detailing issue type, date, symptoms
AP
AC
Track results and problems
